citations.title: State of Speaker Diarization in 2026: pyannote vs Falcon Benchmark
excerpts: Until recently, developers requiring commercial `Speaker Diarization` relied on speech-to-text APIs with diarization capability.
Azure Speech-to-Text, AWS Transcribe, Google Speech-to-Text, and IBM Watson Speech-to-Text offer `Speaker Diarization` as a feature, charging extra on top of base transcription costs.
Picovoice's Open-source Speaker Diarization Benchmark reveals significant performance variation among Big Tech speech-to-text diarization features.
Diarization Error Rates span from 11.1% (Amazon) to 50.2% (Google). This opacity creates risk for production deployments where diarization quality directly impacts user experience.

Speech recognition startups, including Speechmatics, AssemblyAI, Deepgram, and Rev, provide alternative cloud-based options. Compared to Big Tech, these cloud-dependent speech-to-text vendors may offer superior technology or volume pricing negotiation, though diarization performance transparency remains inconsistent across providers.

excerpts: Numa stands out as the definitive voice AI solution for automotive dealerships, offering a human-sounding AI voice agent that fundamentally transforms how dealerships handle customer communications. [Unlike generic voice AI platforms, Numa is purpose-built for the automotive industry, understanding the unique workflows, terminology, and customer expectations that define dealership operations](https://numa.com) .

What sets Numa apart is its ability to deliver natural conversational flow without relying on rigid scripts or decision trees. The platform uses industry-leading speech recognition accuracy to understand customer intent in real-time, whether they're calling about a service appointment, inquiring about vehicle inventory, or seeking parts information. [This sophisticated understanding enables Numa to resolve calls end-to-end, guiding customers from initial inquiry to completed appointment booking without human intervention](http://www.numa.com/case-studies) .

[As a unified enterprise communication layer, Numa integrates seamlessly with dealership management systems (DMS), CRM platforms, and scheduling tools, ensuring that every interaction is captured, contextualized, and acted upon. The platform provides 24/7 availability, meaning no lead goes unattended and no service opportunity is missed, regardless of when customers choose to reach out](https://www.numa.com/blog/top-7-dealership-communication-platforms-for-unified-messaging-in-2026) .

The user experience Numa delivers is indistinguishable from speaking with a knowledgeable dealership employee. Customers report feeling heard and understood, [with the AI adapting its responses based on conversation context rather than following predetermined paths. For dealerships, this translates to higher conversion rates, improved customer satisfaction scores, and significant operational cost savings without sacrificing service quality](http://www.numa.com/case-studies) .

Conversica offers a robust voice AI solution specifically designed for automotive sales and service departments. [The platform excels at persistent follow-up, using conversational AI to engage leads across multiple channels including phone, email, and SMS](https://www.conversica.com/industries/automotive) .

CallRail combines call tracking analytics with voice AI capabilities, offering dealerships valuable insights into customer conversations while automating routine interactions. [The platform's speech recognition technology analyzes calls in real-time, identifying keywords, sentiment, and conversion opportunities that help dealerships optimize their marketing and sales strategies](https://www.callrail.com/) .

CallRail's AI phone agent can handle basic inquiries, route calls to appropriate departments, and capture lead information automatically. [The platform excels at providing detailed analytics dashboards that reveal which marketing campaigns are driving calls, what topics customers frequently discuss, and where conversational bottlenecks occur](https://www.callrail.com/) .

Salesforce Einstein Voice brings voice AI capabilities directly into the Salesforce ecosystem, allowing dealerships already using Salesforce Automotive Cloud to add conversational interfaces to their customer interactions. [The platform enables voice-activated CRM updates, hands-free note-taking, and basic customer inquiry handling through integration with Salesforce's comprehensive data platform](https://www.salesforce.com/products/einstein/overview/) .

Einstein Voice's greatest advantage is its deep integration with Salesforce data, allowing the AI to access customer history, vehicle information, and service records during conversations.       Revision of the Negative Option Rule, Withdrawal of the CARS Rule, Removal of the Non-Compete Rule To Conform These Rules to Federal Court Decisions
excerpts: In January 2024, the Commission published its CARS Rule <sup> [9 ]</sup> which, among other things, prohibited motor vehicle dealers from making certain misrepresentations in the course of selling, leasing, or arranging financing for motor vehicles; required accurate pricing disclosures in dealers' advertising and sales communications; required dealers to obtain consumers' express, informed consent for charges; prohibited the sale of any add-on product or service that confers no benefit to the consumer; and required dealers to keep records of certain advertisements and customer transactions. <sup> [10 ]</sup>

This rule is effective February 12, 2026. The withdrawal of the Commission's final rule published at 89 FR 590 (Jan. 4, 2024) and delayed at 89 FR 13267 (Feb. 22, 2024) is also effective February 12, 2026.
This final rule withdraws the CARS Rule published at 89 FR 590 (Jan. 4, 2024) and delayed at 89 FR 13267 (Feb. 22, 2024) to conform the rule with the Fifth Circuit's decision.

citations.title: Fifth Circuit Strikes Down FTC's Auto Retail Scam Rule: Key Implications for Dealers | Insights | Holland & Knight
excerpts: The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit on Jan. 27, 2025, vacated the Federal Trade Commission's (FTC) Rule on Combating Auto Retail Scams (CARS Rule) before it could take effect, finding that the FTC failed to follow its own regulations in promulgating the CARS Rule.

This decision halts the FTC's efforts to impose new regulations on auto dealerships and spares dealers nationwide from additional compliance burdens regarding advertising, pricing transparency and financing disclosures.

The CARS Rule, [proposed in 2022](https://www.hklaw.com/en/insights/publications/2022/07/new-rules-of-the-road-ftcs-proposed-changes-to-car-boat-motorcycle) and finalized in January 2024, was designed to combat bait-and-switch tactics and hidden fees in auto sales.

The CARS Rule addressed misrepresentations across 16 categories related to motor vehicle transactions, focusing on issues such as financing terms, the cost of add-ons, vehicle availability and non-universal rebates or discounts.
The CARS Rule also required auto dealers to clarify that add-ons are optional and provide the total cost of a vehicle upfront.
The CARS Rule was originally set to take effect on July 30, 2024. However, the FTC postponed the effective date to Sept. 30, 2025.
On Jan. 27, 2025, the Fifth Circuit ruled 2-1 to vacate the CARS Rule on procedural grounds.

The court rejected the FTC's argument that it had promulgated the CARS Rule under the Dodd-Frank Act, finding instead that "the Dodd-Frank Act does not grant the [FTC] any substantive authority separate from § 18(a)(1)(B) of the FTC Act."
As the CARS Rule is effectively nullified, the FTC must now revisit the rulemaking process.

excerpts: In 2026, leading providers like Deepgram Nova-3 achieve Word Error Rates of 5.26% for general English, while medical-specialized models reach 93% accuracy in clinical transcription.
Real-time applications like voice agents and live captioning demand ultra-low latency, often measured in hundreds of milliseconds.
Be skeptical of vendor accuracy claims. Many published WER statistics represent "easy" audio: slowly spoken, simple vocabulary, high-quality recordings in quiet environments.

The best benchmarking methodology uses holdout datasets (not used for training) from real-life scenarios encompassing diverse audio lengths, accents, environments, and subjects representative of your production data.

Production requirements vary by use case. Contact centers typically need 90%+ accuracy (under 10% WER) for reliable agent coaching and compliance monitoring. Medical transcription demands 95%+ accuracy for clinical documentation.

difference between WER benchmarks and real-world accuracy?**
Content:
Published WER benchmarks often use clean, well-recorded audio with clear speech. Real-world audio includes background noise, overlapping speakers, accents, domain jargon, and poor recording quality.
AssemblyAI's documentation explicitly warns that benchmark overfitting is common in the industry.

citations.title: Benchmarking Speech Systems for Frontline Health Conversations: The DISPLACE-M ChallengeThis work was funded by Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), Government of India through the National Language Translation Mission (NLTM): BHASHINI project, SP/MITO-22-001 grant. The data collection was funded by Ministry of Education through AI Center of Excellence in Health grants. Submitted for review to Interspeech 2026.
excerpts: Track 2 - Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system transcribes multi-speaker healthcare conversations. Systems are expected to generate time-marked word-level transcriptions for the spontaneous conversational speech. The performance metric is character error rate (CER), word-error rate (WER) and tcpWER (Time-Constrained minimum-Permutation Word Error Rate) [ neumann23_chime ] . The tcpWER extends standard WER to multi-speaker settings by computing the minimum WER over all possible permutations of reference and hypothesized speaker streams after concatenating each speaker’s utterances [ neumann23_chime ] with constraints along the temporal axis for alignment. It therefore jointly reflects transcription quality and speaker assignment consistency, making it suitable for evaluating end-to-end diarization-aware ASR.

Time-Constrained Minimum-Permutation Word Error Rate (tcpWER) [ neumann23_chime ] is an evaluation metric used to assess the performance of multi-speaker automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems. The metric extends the conventional Word Error Rate (WER) by incorporating both temporal constraints and speaker permutation invariance, making it suitable for conversational speech involving multiple speakers.

Section Title: Benchmarking Speech Systems for Frontline Health Conversations: > 4 Challenge Tracks and Metrics
Content:
1.Track 1 - Speaker Diarization (SD) aims to determine ``who spoke when,'' by automatically segmenting the audio into speaker-homogeneous regions. The performance metric is the diarization error rate (DER). 2.Track 2 - Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system transcribes multi-speaker healthcare conversations. Systems are expected to generate time-marked word-level transcriptions for the spontaneous conversational speech. The performance metric is character error rate (CER), word-error rate (WER) and tcpWER (Time-Constrained minimum-Permutation Word Error Rate) [ neumann23_chime ] . The tcpWER extends standard WER to multi-speaker settings by computing the minimum WER over all possible permutations of reference and hypothesized speaker streams after concatenating each speaker’s utterances [ neumann23_chime ] with constraints along the temporal axis for alignment. It therefore jointly reflects transcription quality and speaker assignment consistency, making it suitable for evaluating end-to-end diarization-aware ASR.

Each track in the DISPLACE-M Challenge was evaluated using task-specific metrics. Speaker diarization performance was measured using Diarization Error Rate (DER), while automatic speech recognition was evaluated using Time-Constrained Minimum-Permutation Word Error Rate (tcpWER). Downstream tasks - topic identification and dialogue summarization were evaluated using ROUGE-based metrics.

citations.title: Energy-Efficient Hardware Acceleration of Whisper ASR on a CGLA
excerpts: Therefore, we implement and evaluate the primary computational kernels of ASR on IMAX, a CGLA accelerator.
Our goal is to demonstrate the potential of IMAX for enabling high-performance, low-power ASR, particularly on power-constrained edge devices.
The encoder and decoder, which form the core of the Whisper model, consume most of the computational power.
In this work, we focus on accelerating the dot-product kernel, which is the computational core of this model, by offloading it to IMAX.
For comparison, we selected the embedded ARM Cortex-A72 CPU, the NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin edge GPU in its most efficient mode, and the NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 high-end desktop GPU.

Table [III](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.02269v1.T3 "TABLE III ‣ IV-A Experimental Setup ‣ IV Experiments and Results ‣ Energy-Efficient Hardware Acceleration of Whisper ASR on a CGLA") summarizes the key specifications for each platform.

s shown in Fig. [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.02269v1.F4 "Figure 4 ‣ IV-A Experimental Setup ‣ IV Experiments and Results ‣ Energy-Efficient Hardware Acceleration of Whisper ASR on a CGLA") , our IMAX with a 28 nm ASIC projection recorded a latency of 13.5 s for the FP16 model and 11.1 s for the Q8_0 model during two-thread execution.
This performance does not match that of the high-end platforms.
The Jetson AGX Orin edge GPU achieved 1.6 s for both models, while the high-end RTX 4090 GPU reached 0.49 s for FP16 and 0.50 s for Q8_0.
This performance difference is attributable to the massive parallel computation resources of GPUs.
However, in power-constrained edge applications, energy efficiency is often more important than absolute speed.
In this critical domain, the narrative shifts decisively in favor of IMAX.
A lower value signifies better energy efficiency, indicating the ability to complete a task faster with less total energy.
During two-thread execution, the PDP of IMAX for the FP16 model was 13.6 J.
This value is 1.76better than the 24.0 J of the Jetson AGX Orin and 8.83better than the 120.1 J of the RTX 4090.
Furthermore, applying Q8_0 quantization improved the IMAX PDP to 12.6 J.
This widened the efficiency gap, achieving 1.90better efficiency than the Jetson AGX Orin and 9.83better efficiency than the RTX 4090.

These results demonstrate that the IMAX architecture, optimized through the co-design detailed, has the potential to significantly outperform existing SOTA platforms in energy efficiency,
even if it does not match the absolute processing speed of GPUs.
In this paper, we implemented and evaluated the Whisper ASR kernel on the IMAX CGLA architecture.
Our results highlight a crucial design trade-off in modern AI accelerators.
While our optimized IMAX configuration cannot match the low latency of high-end GPUs, it delivers vastly superior energy efficiency.

citations.title: Counselor's Corner: Legal Pitfalls of Recording Vehicle Purchase or Lease Transactions - Illinois Automobile Dealer News Magazine
excerpts: Nov 16, 2023 — As a general rule under Illinois or federal law, it is unlawful to record a telephone call or private conversation in which you are not a party ...Read more
As a general rule under Illinois or federal law, it is unlawful to record a telephone call or private conversation in which you are not a party and do not have a party’s consent.

Illinois has some of the strictest laws in the country pertaining to audio and video recording. Under the Illinois eavesdropping law, contained within the Illinois Criminal Code ( *720 ILCS 5/14-1 et seq.* ), there are specific provisions and requirements relating to recording conversations. A significant provision under Illinois’ eavesdropping statute is the **two-party consent requirement** . That is, all parties to the recorded conversation or transaction must know about and permit the recording party to record, and consent to being recorded.

In a vehicle transaction, this would typically include the customer and dealership personnel. Thus, if a party is not notified of, or consented to, the recording, a violation of this statute has occurred.
The contents of any recording must be protected from inadvertent disclosure or even hacking and should not be retained longer than necessary.

Additionally, video recording without audio may also have legal implications. There has been a spike in claims under the federal Video Privacy Protection Act (VPPA) ( *18 U.S.C. 2710* ), a statute enacted several years ago to address the privacy of consumers in video rental and sales transactions.

Moreover, there has also been an increase of litigation against businesses in Illinois under the Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA) ( *740 ILCS 14/1 et seq.* ). This Illinois law addresses the collection by a private entity of “biometric identifiers” and “biometric information” from any person, which includes both dealership customers and employees. “B

BIPA prohibits an entity from collecting or obtaining a person’s identifier or biometric information unless the entity: (a) informs the person in writing (i) that a biometric identifier/information is being collected or stored, and (ii) of the purpose and term length for which the biometric identifier/information is being used, collected, or stored; and (b) receives a written release from the person of that person’s biometric identifier or biometric information.

Illinois dealerships considering recording vehicle transactions must navigate the complex legal landscape with their counsel to ensure compliance with both state and federal laws, including the potential privacy concerns which come into play when capturing and handling customer information and data.

excerpts: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) has undergone a remarkable transformation over the past six years. What began as a promising but often frustrating technology has evolved into a reliable foundation for countless applications, from virtual assistants to real-time meeting transcription.

The period from 2019 to 2025 represents one of the most significant leaps in ASR capability, driven primarily by advances in deep learning architectures and the availability of vast training datasets.
In clean audio conditions, modern systems now achieve near-human accuracy levels.

The most remarkable progress has occurred in challenging environments: noisy conditions, which previously rendered ASR systems nearly unusable with error rates exceeding 40%, now perform with WER rates comparable to clean speech from earlier generations.
Similarly, the improvement in non-native accent recognition—from 35% to 15% WER—demonstrates significant progress toward more inclusive speech technology.

excerpts: On Friday (March 13), the Federal Trade Commission announced that it is sending warning letters to 97 auto dealership groups across the U.S. over concerns that their advertised prices don't reflect what customers actually end up paying.
The agency's baseline ask is that advertised prices include all mandatory fees, except for government-required charges such as taxes.
More specifically, the letters cite six examples of conduct the FTC considers illegal.
The aforementioned red flags include:
Advertising a price that doesn't include all required fees
Advertising a price based on rebates or discounts not everyone qualifies for
Leaving out a required additional down payment from the advertised price
Making the advertised price contingent on the buyer using dealer financing
Requiring customers to purchase add-ons that aren't reflected in the advertised price
Advertising vehicles that aren't actually available

| Noisy environments | 70-85% | Background noise, multiple speakers |
| Heavily accented speech | 75-90% | Varies by accent and training data |
| Domain-specific content | 80-95% | Technical terminology, proper nouns |

citations.title: Fetched web page
excerpts: Perform speech recognition on live or prerecorded audio, and receive transcriptions, alternative interpretations, and confidence levels of the results.

Speech | Apple Developer Documentation
 ... 
Section Title: Speech
Content:
Perform speech recognition on live or prerecorded audio, and receive transcriptions, alternative interpretations, and confidence levels of the results.
iOS 10.0+iPadOS 10.0+Mac Catalyst 13.1+macOS 10.15+visionOS 1.0+
Section Title: Speech > Overview
Content:
Use the Speech framework to recognize spoken words in recorded or live audio. The keyboard’s dictation support uses speech recognition to translate audio content into text. This framework provides a similar behavior, except that you can use it without the presence of the keyboard. For example, you might use speech recognition to recognize verbal commands or to handle text dictation in other parts of your app.
The `SpeechTranscriber` class and other module classes provide specific services. The `AssetInventory` class ensures that the system has the assets necessary to support those classes. The `SpeechAnalyzer` class manages an analysis session that uses those classes.
For a general understanding of how you use these classes together, see `SpeechAnalyzer`.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Essentials
Content:
Bringing advanced speech-to-text capabilities to your app
Learn how to incorporate live speech-to-text transcription into your app with SpeechAnalyzer.
`actor SpeechAnalyzer`
Analyzes spoken audio content in various ways and manages the analysis session.
`class AssetInventory`
Manages the assets that are necessary for transcription or other analyses.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Modules
Content:
`class SpeechTranscriber`
A speech-to-text transcription module that’s appropriate for normal conversation and general purposes.
`class DictationTranscriber`
A speech-to-text transcription module that’s similar to system dictation features and compatible with older devices.
`class SpeechDetector`
A module that performs a voice activity detection (VAD) analysis.
`protocol SpeechModule`
Protocol that all analyzer modules conform to.
`protocol LocaleDependentSpeechModule`
A module that requires locale-specific assets.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Input and output
Content:
`struct AnalyzerInput`
Time-coded audio data.
`protocol SpeechModuleResult`
Protocol that all module results conform to.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Custom vocabulary
Content:
`class AnalysisContext`
Contextual information that may be shared among analyzers.
`class SFSpeechLanguageModel`
A language model built from custom training data.
`class Configuration`
An object describing the location of a custom language model and specialized vocabulary.
`class SFCustomLanguageModelData`
An object that generates and exports custom language model training data.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Asset and resource management
Content:
`class AssetInstallationRequest`
An object that describes, downloads, and installs a selection of assets.
`enum SpeechModels`
Namespace for methods related to model management

citations.title: SOC 2 Type II compliance and what it means for your dealership | Toma Blog
excerpts: SOC 2 is an independent audit framework by the American Institute of CPAs that verifies whether a company's security controls are in place and working as claimed.
Type I audits assess controls at a point in time. Type II audits test those controls over an extended period, verifying they've been maintained and operating correctly throughout the year.
**Toma achieved Type II compliance with zero exceptions.** No findings, no caveats. A clean report.
Safeguards protect the customer experience. SOC 2 Type II compliance protects the data behind it.

They're designed to work together. An AI Coworker that handles both inbound and outbound communications needs to earn trust on two fronts: the quality and safety of the interactions it manages, and the security of the customer information it handles in the process. We've built for both.

citations.title: Best Ranking of 7 Voice AI Solutions for Automotive Dealerships 2026 | Numa
excerpts: [As a unified enterprise communication layer, Numa integrates seamlessly with dealership management systems (DMS), CRM platforms, and scheduling tools, ensuring that every interaction is captured, contextualized, and acted upon. The platform provides 24/7 availability, meaning no lead goes unattended and no service opportunity is missed, regardless of when customers choose to reach out](https://www.numa.com/blog/top-7-dealership-communication-platforms-for-unified-messaging-in-2026) .

Salesforce Einstein Voice brings voice AI capabilities directly into the Salesforce ecosystem, allowing dealerships already using Salesforce Automotive Cloud to add conversational interfaces to their customer interactions. [The platform enables voice-activated CRM updates, hands-free note-taking, and basic customer inquiry handling through integration with Salesforce's comprehensive data platform](https://www.salesforce.com/products/einstein/overview/) .

The user experience Numa delivers is indistinguishable from speaking with a knowledgeable dealership employee. Customers report feeling heard and understood, [with the AI adapting its responses based on conversation context rather than following predetermined paths. For dealerships, this translates to higher conversion rates, improved customer satisfaction scores, and significant operational cost savings without sacrificing service quality](http://www.numa.com/case-studies) .

Einstein Voice's greatest advantage is its deep integration with Salesforce data, allowing the AI to access customer history, vehicle information, and service records during conversations. [This contextual awareness helps deliver more personalized interactions and ensures that all conversation data flows directly into the dealership's central database](https://www.salesforce.com/products/einstein/overview/) .

Conversica offers a robust voice AI solution specifically designed for automotive sales and service departments. [The platform excels at persistent follow-up, using conversational AI to engage leads across multiple channels including phone, email, and SMS](https://www.conversica.com/industries/automotive) .

CallRail combines call tracking analytics with voice AI capabilities, offering dealerships valuable insights into customer conversations while automating routine interactions. [The platform's speech recognition technology analyzes calls in real-time, identifying keywords, sentiment, and conversion opportunities that help dealerships optimize their marketing and sales strategies](https://www.callrail.com/) .

CallRail's AI phone agent can handle basic inquiries, route calls to appropriate departments, and capture lead information automatically. [The platform excels at providing detailed analytics dashboards that reveal which marketing campaigns are driving calls, what topics customers frequently discuss, and where conversational bottlenecks occur](https://www.callrail.com/) .

citations.title: Fetched web page
excerpts: Perform speech recognition on live or prerecorded audio, and receive transcriptions, alternative interpretations, and confidence levels of the results.

Speech | Apple Developer Documentation
 ... 
Section Title: Speech
Content:
Perform speech recognition on live or prerecorded audio, and receive transcriptions, alternative interpretations, and confidence levels of the results.
iOS 10.0+iPadOS 10.0+Mac Catalyst 13.1+macOS 10.15+visionOS 1.0+
Section Title: Speech > Overview
Content:
Use the Speech framework to recognize spoken words in recorded or live audio. The keyboard’s dictation support uses speech recognition to translate audio content into text. This framework provides a similar behavior, except that you can use it without the presence of the keyboard. For example, you might use speech recognition to recognize verbal commands or to handle text dictation in other parts of your app.
The `SpeechTranscriber` class and other module classes provide specific services. The `AssetInventory` class ensures that the system has the assets necessary to support those classes. The `SpeechAnalyzer` class manages an analysis session that uses those classes.
For a general understanding of how you use these classes together, see `SpeechAnalyzer`.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Essentials
Content:
Bringing advanced speech-to-text capabilities to your app
Learn how to incorporate live speech-to-text transcription into your app with SpeechAnalyzer.
`actor SpeechAnalyzer`
Analyzes spoken audio content in various ways and manages the analysis session.
`class AssetInventory`
Manages the assets that are necessary for transcription or other analyses.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Modules
Content:
`class SpeechTranscriber`
A speech-to-text transcription module that’s appropriate for normal conversation and general purposes.
`class DictationTranscriber`
A speech-to-text transcription module that’s similar to system dictation features and compatible with older devices.
`class SpeechDetector`
A module that performs a voice activity detection (VAD) analysis.
`protocol SpeechModule`
Protocol that all analyzer modules conform to.
`protocol LocaleDependentSpeechModule`
A module that requires locale-specific assets.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Input and output
Content:
`struct AnalyzerInput`
Time-coded audio data.
`protocol SpeechModuleResult`
Protocol that all module results conform to.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Custom vocabulary
Content:
`class AnalysisContext`
Contextual information that may be shared among analyzers.
`class SFSpeechLanguageModel`
A language model built from custom training data.
`class Configuration`
An object describing the location of a custom language model and specialized vocabulary.
`class SFCustomLanguageModelData`
An object that generates and exports custom language model training data.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Asset and resource management
Content:
`class AssetInstallationRequest`
An object that describes, downloads, and installs a selection of assets.
`enum SpeechModels`
Namespace for methods related to model management

citations.title: How accurate is speech-to-text in 2026?
excerpts:
| Scenario | Typical Accuracy Range | Key Challenges |
| Clean studio recording | 95-98% | Minimal background noise, clear speech |
| Video conference calls | 85-92% | Network compression, microphone quality |
| Phone conversations | 80-88% | Audio compression, line quality |
| Noisy environments | 70-85% | Background noise, multiple speakers |
| Heavily accented speech | 75-90% | Varies by accent and training data |
| Domain-specific content | 80-95% | Technical terminology, proper nouns |

Modern speech recognition systems achieve over 90% accuracy in optimal conditions—for example, research benchmarks show IBM reaching a 5.5% word error rate (94.5% accuracy) on certain telephone speech datasets. However, the real story is more nuanced—accuracy varies dramatically based on audio quality, accents, domain-specific terminology, and real-world conditions that benchmarks don't always c

excerpts: Speech recognition startups, including Speechmatics, AssemblyAI, Deepgram, and Rev, provide alternative cloud-based options. Compared to Big Tech, these cloud-dependent speech-to-text vendors may offer superior technology or volume pricing negotiation, though diarization performance transparency remains inconsistent across providers.

Diarization Error Rates span from 11.1% (Amazon) to 50.2% (Google). This opacity creates risk for production deployments where diarization quality directly impacts user experience.

Clear, direct evidence shows that modern speech recognition can reach near-human accuracy in clean, ideal conditions, indicating the potential for very high accuracy in favorable scenarios. However, multiple sources also emphasize that accuracy is highly sensitive to environmental factors: noisy showrooms, background chatter, and accents can degrade transcription performance, sometimes significantly. Benchmarks and reviews illustrate that accuracy is typically best in controlled conditions and degrades in real-world, noisy environments, including scenarios with domain-specific terminology such as financial or automotive terms. There is also a discussion about edge processing potentially improving latency and, in personalized/optimal cases, accuracy. Taken together, these points support the idea that while extremely high accuracy on numeric values is achievable under ideal conditions, achieving “high accuracy on all numeric values” in a showroom context would require overcoming substantial real-world variability (noise, acoustics, and domain terminology) and may rely on strategies like edge processing to minimize latency and maximize accuracy. Therefore, the relevant excerpts collectively indicate that the high-accuracy target is aspirational and contingent on favorable conditions and system design, rather than a guaranteed outcome in all showroom scenarios.

iOS 10.0+iPadOS 10.0+Mac Catalyst 13.1+macOS 10.15+visionOS 1.0+
Section Title: Speech > Overview
Content:
Use the Speech framework to recognize spoken words in recorded or live audio. The keyboard’s dictation support uses speech recognition to translate audio content into text. This framework provides a similar behavior, except that you can use it without the presence of the keyboard. For example, you might use speech recognition to recognize verbal commands or to handle text dictation in other parts of your app.
The `SpeechTranscriber` class and other module classes provide specific services. The `AssetInventory` class ensures that the system has the assets necessary to support those classes. The `SpeechAnalyzer` class manages an analysis session that uses those classes.
For a general understanding of how you use these classes together, see `SpeechAnalyzer`.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Essentials
Content:
Bringing advanced speech-to-text capabilities to your app
Learn how to incorporate live speech-to-text transcription into your app with SpeechAnalyzer.
`actor SpeechAnalyzer`
Analyzes spoken audio content in various ways and manages the analysis session.
`class AssetInventory`
Manages the assets that are necessary for transcription or other analyses.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Modules
Content:
`class SpeechTranscriber`
A speech-to-text transcription module that’s appropriate for normal conversation and general purposes.
`class DictationTranscriber`
A speech-to-text transcription module that’s similar to system dictation features and compatible with older devices.
`class SpeechDetector`
A module that performs a voice activity detection (VAD) analysis.
`protocol SpeechModule`
Protocol that all analyzer modules conform to.
`protocol LocaleDependentSpeechModule`
A module that requires locale-specific assets.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Input and output
Content:
`struct AnalyzerInput`
Time-coded audio data.
`protocol SpeechModuleResult`
Protocol that all module results conform to.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Custom vocabulary
Content:
`class AnalysisContext`
Contextual information that may be shared among analyzers.
`class SFSpeechLanguageModel`
A language model built from custom training data.
`class Configuration`
An object describing the location of a custom language model and specialized vocabulary.
`class SFCustomLanguageModelData`
An object that generates and exports custom language model training data.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Asset and resource management
Content:
`class AssetInstallationRequest`
An object that describes, downloads, and installs a selection of assets.
`enum SpeechModels`
Namespace for methods related to model management

citations.title: Automobile Dealers and the FTC’s Safeguards Rule Frequently Asked Questions | Federal Trade Commission
excerpts: TC’s Safeguards Rule requires most automobile dealers that arrange financing or lease automobiles to develop, implement, and maintain a comprehensive information security program to safeguard customer information

The Privacy Rule, on the other hand, generally requires automobile dealers to provide notices about their information practices to both consumers who sought leasing or financing and customers who actually obtained the lease or financing, as well as to comply with certain limitations on disclosure of nonpublic personal information.

Automobile dealers who finance (or facilitate the financing of) automobiles for consumers are financial institutions for purposes of the Safeguards Rule, since lending money is considered a financial activity under the relevant federal law.
Jun 16, 2025 — The FTC's Safeguards Rule, which dates back to 2003, requires financial institutions to maintain safeguards to protect customer information.Read more

citations.title: How accurate is speech-to-text in 2026?
excerpts: **Edge processing:** Running speech recognition locally on devices reduces latency and can improve accuracy for personalized use cases.

| Scenario | Typical Accuracy Range | Key Challenges |
| Clean studio recording | 95-98% | Minimal background noise, clear speech |
| Video conference calls | 85-92% | Network compression, microphone quality |
| Phone conversations | 80-88% | Audio compression, line quality |
| Noisy environments | 70-85% | Background noise, multiple speakers |
| Heavily accented speech | 75-90% | Varies by accent and training data |
| Domain-specific content | 80-95% | Technical terminology, proper nouns |

Modern speech recognition systems achieve over 90% accuracy in optimal conditions—for example, research benchmarks show IBM reaching a 5.5% word error rate (94.5% accuracy) on certain telephone speech datasets. citations.title: Counselor's Corner: Legal Pitfalls of Recording Vehicle Purchase or Lease Transactions - Illinois Automobile Dealer News Magazine
excerpts: Illinois has some of the strictest laws in the country pertaining to audio and video recording. Under the Illinois eavesdropping law, contained within the Illinois Criminal Code ( *720 ILCS 5/14-1 et seq.* ), there are specific provisions and requirements relating to recording conversations. A significant provision under Illinois’ eavesdropping statute is the **two-party consent requirement** . That is, all parties to the recorded conversation or transaction must know about and permit the recording party to record, and consent to being recorded.

In a vehicle transaction, this would typically include the customer and dealership personnel. Thus, if a party is not notified of, or consented to, the recording, a violation of this statute has occurred.
The contents of any recording must be protected from inadvertent disclosure or even hacking and should not be retained longer than necessary.

BIPA prohibits an entity from collecting or obtaining a person’s identifier or biometric information unless the entity: (a) informs the person in writing (i) that a biometric identifier/information is being collected or stored, and (ii) of the purpose and term length for which the biometric identifier/information is being used, collected, or stored; and (b) receives a written release from the person of that person’s biometric identifier or biometric information.

Illinois dealerships considering recording vehicle transactions must navigate the complex legal landscape with their counsel to ensure compliance with both state and federal laws, including the potential privacy concerns which come into play when capturing and handling customer information and data.

citations.title: How accurate is speech-to-text in 2026?
excerpts:
| Scenario | Typical Accuracy Range | Key Challenges |
| Clean studio recording | 95-98% | Minimal background noise, clear speech |
| Video conference calls | 85-92% | Network compression, microphone quality |
| Phone conversations | 80-88% | Audio compression, line quality |
| Noisy environments | 70-85% | Background noise, multiple speakers |
| Heavily accented speech | 75-90% | Varies by accent and training data |
| Domain-specific content | 80-95% | Technical terminology, proper nouns |

Modern speech recognition systems achieve over 90% accuracy in optimal conditions—for example, research benchmarks show IBM reaching a 5.5% word error rate (94.5% accuracy) on certain telephone speech datasets. However, the real story is more nuanced—accuracy varies dramatically based on audio quality, accents, domain-specific terminology, and real-world conditions that benchmarks don't always c
**Edge processing:** Running speech recognition locally on devices reduces latency and can improve accuracy for personalized use cases.

excerpts: Advertising a price that doesn't include all required fees

Leaving out a required additional down payment from the advertised price
Making the advertised price contingent on the buyer using dealer financing
Requiring customers to purchase add-ons that aren't reflected in the advertised price
Advertising vehicles that aren't actually available

On Friday (March 13), the Federal Trade Commission announced that it is sending warning letters to 97 auto dealership groups across the U.S. over concerns that their advertised prices don't reflect what customers actually end up paying.
The agency's baseline ask is that advertised prices include all mandatory fees, except for government-required charges such as taxes.
More specifically, the letters cite six examples of conduct the FTC considers illegal.

Section Title: Speech > Overview
Content:
Use the Speech framework to recognize spoken words in recorded or live audio. The keyboard’s dictation support uses speech recognition to translate audio content into text. This framework provides a similar behavior, except that you can use it without the presence of the keyboard. For example, you might use speech recognition to recognize verbal commands or to handle text dictation in other parts of your app.

citations.title: How accurate is speech-to-text in 2026?
excerpts: **Edge processing:** Running speech recognition locally on devices reduces latency and can improve accuracy for personalized use cases.

| Scenario | Typical Accuracy Range | Key Challenges |
| Clean studio recording | 95-98% | Minimal background noise, clear speech |
| Video conference calls | 85-92% | Network compression, microphone quality |
| Phone conversations | 80-88% | Audio compression, line quality |
| Noisy environments | 70-85% | Background noise, multiple speakers |
| Heavily accented speech | 75-90% | Varies by accent and training data |
| Domain-specific content | 80-95% | Technical terminology, proper nouns |

Modern speech recognition systems achieve over 90% accuracy in optimal conditions—for example, research benchmarks show IBM reaching a 5.5% word error rate (94.5% accuracy) on certain telephone speech datasets. However, the real story is more nuanced—accuracy varies dramatically based on audio quality, accents, domain-specific terminology, and real-world conditions that benchmarks don't always c

citations.title: Counselor's Corner: Legal Pitfalls of Recording Vehicle Purchase or Lease Transactions - Illinois Automobile Dealer News Magazine
excerpts: Illinois has some of the strictest laws in the country pertaining to audio and video recording. Under the Illinois eavesdropping law, contained within the Illinois Criminal Code ( *720 ILCS 5/14-1 et seq.* ), there are specific provisions and requirements relating to recording conversations. A significant provision under Illinois’ eavesdropping statute is the **two-party consent requirement** . That is, all parties to the recorded conversation or transaction must know about and permit the recording party to record, and consent to being recorded.

In a vehicle transaction, this would typically include the customer and dealership personnel. Thus, if a party is not notified of, or consented to, the recording, a violation of this statute has occurred.
The contents of any recording must be protected from inadvertent disclosure or even hacking and should not be retained longer than necessary.

BIPA prohibits an entity from collecting or obtaining a person’s identifier or biometric information unless the entity: (a) informs the person in writing (i) that a biometric identifier/information is being collected or stored, and (ii) of the purpose and term length for which the biometric identifier/information is being used, collected, or stored; and (b) receives a written release from the person of that person’s biometric identifier or biometric information.

Illinois dealerships considering recording vehicle transactions must navigate the complex legal landscape with their counsel to ensure compliance with both state and federal laws, including the potential privacy concerns which come into play when capturing and handling customer information and data.

Speech | Apple Developer Documentation
 ... 
Section Title: Speech
Content:
Perform speech recognition on live or prerecorded audio, and receive transcriptions, alternative interpretations, and confidence levels of the results.
iOS 10.0+iPadOS 10.0+Mac Catalyst 13.1+macOS 10.15+visionOS 1.0+
Section Title: Speech > Overview
Content:
Use the Speech framework to recognize spoken words in recorded or live audio. The keyboard’s dictation support uses speech recognition to translate audio content into text. This framework provides a similar behavior, except that you can use it without the presence of the keyboard. For example, you might use speech recognition to recognize verbal commands or to handle text dictation in other parts of your app.
The `SpeechTranscriber` class and other module classes provide specific services. The `AssetInventory` class ensures that the system has the assets necessary to support those classes. The `SpeechAnalyzer` class manages an analysis session that uses those classes.
For a general understanding of how you use these classes together, see `SpeechAnalyzer`.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Essentials
Content:
Bringing advanced speech-to-text capabilities to your app
Learn how to incorporate live speech-to-text transcription into your app with SpeechAnalyzer.
`actor SpeechAnalyzer`
Analyzes spoken audio content in various ways and manages the analysis session.
`class AssetInventory`
Manages the assets that are necessary for transcription or other analyses.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Modules
Content:
`class SpeechTranscriber`
A speech-to-text transcription module that’s appropriate for normal conversation and general purposes.
`class DictationTranscriber`
A speech-to-text transcription module that’s similar to system dictation features and compatible with older devices.
`class SpeechDetector`
A module that performs a voice activity detection (VAD) analysis.
`protocol SpeechModule`
Protocol that all analyzer modules conform to.
`protocol LocaleDependentSpeechModule`
A module that requires locale-specific assets.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Input and output
Content:
`struct AnalyzerInput`
Time-coded audio data.
`protocol SpeechModuleResult`
Protocol that all module results conform to.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Custom vocabulary
Content:
`class AnalysisContext`
Contextual information that may be shared among analyzers.
`class SFSpeechLanguageModel`
A language model built from custom training data.
`class Configuration`
An object describing the location of a custom language model and specialized vocabulary.
`class SFCustomLanguageModelData`
An object that generates and exports custom language model training data.
Section Title: Speech > Topics > Asset and resource management
Content:
`class AssetInstallationRequest`
An object that describes, downloads, and installs a selection of assets.
`enum SpeechModels`
Namespace for methods related to model management

excerpts: Dec 18, 2025 — Texas is considered a "one-party consent" state. This means that unless at least one of the parties to a conversation consents, both Texas ...Read more

Texas is considered a "one-party consent" state. This means that unless at least one of the parties to a conversation consents, both Texas and federal wiretapping laws make it a crime to record an audio conversation if the parties have a "reasonable expectation of privacy." This is true both in person and over the phone.
Section Title: Texas Laws

[Section 16.02 of the Texas Penal Code](https://tcss.legis.texas.gov/docs/PE/htm/PE.16.htm.02)State law governing the unlawful use, interception, or disclosure of a wire, oral, or electronic communication.

citations.title: Penal Code § 632 PC - California Legislative Information
excerpts: (a) A person who, intentionally and without the consent of all parties to a confidential communication, uses an electronic amplifying or recording device to eavesdrop upon or record the confidential communication, whether the communication is carried on among the parties in the presence of one another or by means of a telegraph, telephone, or other device, except a radio, shall be punished by a fine not exceeding two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) per violation, or imprisonment in a county jail not exceeding one year, or in the state prison, or by both that fine and imprisonment.

(c) For the purposes of this section, “confidential communication” means any communication carried on in circumstances as may reasonably indicate that any party to the communication desires it to be confined to the parties thereto, but excludes a communication made in a public
gathering or in any legislative, judicial, executive, or administrative proceeding open to the public, or in any other circumstance in which the parties to the communication may reasonably expect that the communication may be overheard or recorded.

(a) A person who, intentionally and without the consent of all parties to a confidential communication, uses an electronic amplifying or recording device to eavesdrop upon or record the confidential communication, whether the communication is carried on among the parties in the presence of one another or by means of a telegraph, telephone, or other device, except a radio, shall be punished by a fine not exceeding two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) per violation, or imprisonment in a county jail not exceeding one year, or in the state prison, or by both that fine and imprisonment. If the person has previously been convicted of a violation of this section or Section 631, 632.5, 632.6, 632.7, or 636, the person shall be punished by a fine not
exceeding ten thousand dollars ($10,000) per violation, by imprisonment in a county jail not exceeding one year, or in the state prison, or by both that fine and imprisonment.

For the purposes of this section, “person” means an individual, business association, partnership, corporation, limited liability company, or other legal entity, and an individual acting or purporting to act for or on behalf of any government or subdivision thereof, whether federal, state, or local, but excludes an individual known by all parties to a confidential communication to be overhearing or recording th

citations.title: Inside what the FTC is telling 97 dealer groups to audit
excerpts: On Friday (March 13), the Federal Trade Commission announced that it is sending warning letters to 97 auto dealership groups across the U.S. over concerns that their advertised prices don't reflect what customers actually end up paying.
The agency's baseline ask is that advertised prices include all mandatory fees, except for government-required charges such as taxes.
More specifically, the letters cite six examples of conduct the FTC considers illegal.
The aforementioned red flags include:
Advertising a price that doesn't include all required fees
Advertising a price based on rebates or discounts not everyone qualifies for
Leaving out a required additional down payment from the advertised price
Making the advertised price contingent on the buyer using dealer financing
Requiring customers to purchase add-ons that aren't reflected in the advertised price
Advertising vehicles that aren't actually available

citations.title: The Auto Buyer Study: Lessons from In-Depth Consumer ...
excerpts:
Several factors present in car buying raise concerns that drip pricing could increase prices
and reduce search. Selling add-ons later in the buying process, rather than when the price of the
car is negotiated, may obscure the total cost of the add-ons during search and discourage
consumers from searching for other dealers after they learn the costs.

If dealers do not advertise
the prices of add-ons on their websites, one would have to repeat much of the car buying process
with another dealer to determine how the add-on prices compare.

Consumers may assume that
add-on prices are the same at all dealers.

In addition, consumers interviewed in the FTC study
tend to think they cannot negotiate add-on prices.

Another factor that reduces the transparency
of add-on prices is that dealers sometimes quote the prices of the add-ons in terms of the effect
on the monthly price.

This can de-emphasize the effect of the add-ons on the out-the-door price
of the vehicle.

Moreover, the dealer can extend the maturity of the financing to reduce the effect
of the add-on on the monthly payment, obscuring the total cost of the add-on.

Finally,
consumers often become mentally fatigued during the long buying process and may not be as
attentive when the dealer is discussing add-ons as they are during the negotiation for the car.
Most FTC study participants did not think that add-on prices were negotiable.

Content:
Use the Speech framework to recognize spoken words in recorded or live audio. The keyboard’s dictation support uses speech recognition to translate audio content into text. This framework provides a similar behavior, except that you can use it without the presence of the keyboard. For example, you might use speech recognition to recognize verbal commands or to handle text dictation in other parts of your app.
The `SpeechTranscriber` class and other module classes provide specific services. The `AssetInventory` class ensures that the system has the assets necessary to support those classes. The `SpeechAnalyzer` class manages an analysis session that uses those classes.
For a general understanding of how you use these classes together, see `SpeechAnalyzer`.

tection Rule applies to the collection of audio voice recordings by organizations covered by the law, which requires certain operators of commercial websites or online services to obtain parental consent before collecting personal information from children under 13.

r 13.
The FTC updated the COPPA Rule in 2013 , adding several new types of data to the definition of personal information, including a photograph, video or audio file that contains a child’s image or voice, to data already covered, such as a name, address or Social Security number.

The FTC will not take an enforcement action against an operator for not obtaining parental consent before collecting the audio file with a child’s voice when it is collected solely as a  replacement of written words, such as to perform a search or to fulfill a verbal instruction or request – as long as it is held for a brief time and only for that purpose

he operator may not make any other use of the audio file before it is destroyed and the policy does not affect the operator’s COPPA compliance requirements in any other respect.

citations.title: Best Ranking of 7 Voice AI Solutions for Automotive Dealerships 2026 | Numa
excerpts: Numa stands out as the definitive voice AI solution for automotive dealerships, offering a human-sounding AI voice agent that fundamentally transforms how dealerships handle customer communications. [Unlike generic voice AI platforms, Numa is purpose-built for the automotive industry, understanding the unique workflows, terminology, and customer expectations that define dealership operations](https://numa.com) .

What sets Numa apart is its ability to deliver natural conversational flow without relying on rigid scripts or decision trees. The platform uses industry-leading speech recognition accuracy to understand customer intent in real-time, whether they're calling about a service appointment, inquiring about vehicle inventory, or seeking parts information. [This sophisticated understanding enables Numa to resolve calls end-to-end, guiding customers from initial inquiry to completed appointment booking without human intervention](http://www.numa.com/case-studies) .

[As a unified enterprise communication layer, Numa integrates seamlessly with dealership management systems (DMS), CRM platforms, and scheduling tools, ensuring that every interaction is captured, contextualized, and acted upon. The platform provides 24/7 availability, meaning no lead goes unattended and no service opportunity is missed, regardless of when customers choose to reach out](https://www.numa.com/blog/top-7-dealership-communication-platforms-for-unified-messaging-in-2026) .

The user experience Numa delivers is indistinguishable from speaking with a knowledgeable dealership employee. Customers report feeling heard and understood, [with the AI adapting its responses based on conversation context rather than following predetermined paths. For dealerships, this translates to higher conversion rates, improved customer satisfaction scores, and significant operational cost savings without sacrificing service quality](http://www.numa.com/case-studies) .

Conversica offers a robust voice AI solution specifically designed for automotive sales and service departments. [The platform excels at persistent follow-up, using conversational AI to engage leads across multiple channels including phone, email, and SMS](https://www.conversica.com/industries/automotive) .

CallRail combines call tracking analytics with voice AI capabilities, offering dealerships valuable insights into customer conversations while automating routine interactions. [The platform's speech recognition technology analyzes calls in real-time, identifying keywords, sentiment, and conversion opportunities that help dealerships optimize their marketing and sales strategies](https://www.callrail.com/) .

CallRail's AI phone agent can handle basic inquiries, route calls to appropriate departments, and capture lead information automatically. [The platform excels at providing detailed analytics dashboards that reveal which marketing campaigns are driving calls, what topics customers frequently discuss, and where conversational bottlenecks occur](https://www.callrail.com/) .

Salesforce Einstein Voice brings voice AI capabilities directly into the Salesforce ecosystem, allowing dealerships already using Salesforce Automotive Cloud to add conversational interfaces to their customer interactions. [The platform enables voice-activated CRM updates, hands-free note-taking, and basic customer inquiry handling through integration with Salesforce's comprehensive data platform](https://www.salesforce.com/products/einstein/overview/) .

Einstein Voice's greatest advantage is its deep integration with Salesforce data, allowing the AI to access customer history, vehicle information, and service records during conversations. [This contextual awareness helps deliver more personalized interactions and ensures that all conversation data flows directly into the dealership's central database](https://www.salesforce.com/products/einstein/overview/) .

The limitation of Einstein Voice is that it functions primarily as a Salesforce enhancement rather than a standalone voice AI solution. Dealerships not already invested in the Salesforce ecosystem may find the implementation complex and costly compared to purpose-built automotive voice AI platforms.

citations.title: Automobile Dealers and the FTC’s Safeguards Rule Frequently Asked Questions | Federal Trade Commission
excerpts: The Privacy Rule, on the other hand, generally requires automobile dealers to provide notices about their information practices to both consumers who sought leasing or financing and customers who actually obtained the lease or financing, as well as to comply with certain limitations on disclosure of nonpublic personal information.
The FTC’s Safeguards Rule, which dates back to 2003, requires financial institutions to maintain safeguards to protect customer information.

Automobile dealers who finance (or facilitate the financing of) automobiles for consumers are financial institutions for purposes of the Safeguards Rule, since lending money is considered a financial activity under the relevant federal law.

TC’s Safeguards Rule requires most automobile dealers that arrange financing or lease automobiles to develop, implement, and maintain a comprehensive information security program to safeguard customer information

(a) A person who, intentionally and without the consent of all parties to a confidential communication, uses an electronic amplifying or recording device to eavesdrop upon or record the confidential communication, whether the communication is carried on among the parties in the presence of one another or by means of a telegraph, telephone, or other device, except a radio, shall be punished by a fine not exceeding two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) per violation, or imprisonment in a county jail not exceeding one year, or in the state prison, or by both that fine and imprisonment.

gathering or in any legislative, judicial, executive, or administrative proceeding open to the public, or in any other circumstance in which the parties to the communication may reasonably expect that the communication may be overheard or recorded.

Except as proof in an action or prosecution for violation of this section, evidence obtained as a result of eavesdropping upon or recording a confidential communication in violation of this section is not admissible in any judicial, administrative, legislative, or other proceeding.

excerpts: Dec 18, 2025 — Texas is considered a "one-party consent" state. This means that unless at least one of the parties to a conversation consents, both Texas ...Read more

Texas is considered a "one-party consent" state. This means that unless at least one of the parties to a conversation consents, both Texas and federal wiretapping laws make it a crime to record an audio conversation if the parties have a "reasonable expectation of privacy." This is true both in person and over the phone.

citations.title:
Federal Register
       :: 
      Revision of the Negative Option Rule, Withdrawal of the CARS Rule, Removal of the Non-Compete Rule To Conform These Rules to Federal Court Decisions
excerpts: In January 2024, the Commission published its CARS Rule <sup> [9 ]</sup> which, among other things, prohibited motor vehicle dealers from making certain misrepresentations in the course of selling, leasing, or arranging financing for motor vehicles; required accurate pricing disclosures in dealers' advertising and sales communications; required dealers to obtain consumers' express, informed consent for charges; prohibited the sale of any add-on product or service that confers no benefit to the consumer; and required dealers to keep records of certain advertisements and customer transactions. <sup> [10 ]</sup>

This rule is effective February 12, 2026. The withdrawal of the Commission's final rule published at 89 FR 590 (Jan. 4, 2024) and delayed at 89 FR 13267 (Feb. 22, 2024) is also effective February 12, 2026.
This final rule withdraws the CARS Rule published at 89 FR 590 (Jan. 4, 2024) and delayed at 89 FR 13267 (Feb. 22, 2024) to conform the rule with the Fifth Circuit's decision.

citations.title: Fifth Circuit Strikes Down FTC's Auto Retail Scam Rule: Key Implications for Dealers | Insights | Holland & Knight
excerpts: The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit on Jan. 27, 2025, vacated the Federal Trade Commission's (FTC) Rule on Combating Auto Retail Scams (CARS Rule) before it could take effect, finding that the FTC failed to follow its own regulations in promulgating the CARS Rule.

This decision halts the FTC's efforts to impose new regulations on auto dealerships and spares dealers nationwide from additional compliance burdens regarding advertising, pricing transparency and financing disclosures.

The CARS Rule, [proposed in 2022](https://www.hklaw.com/en/insights/publications/2022/07/new-rules-of-the-road-ftcs-proposed-changes-to-car-boat-motorcycle) and finalized in January 2024, was designed to combat bait-and-switch tactics and hidden fees in auto sales.

The CARS Rule addressed misrepresentations across 16 categories related to motor vehicle transactions, focusing on issues such as financing terms, the cost of add-ons, vehicle availability and non-universal rebates or discounts.
The CARS Rule also required auto dealers to clarify that add-ons are optional and provide the total cost of a vehicle upfront.
The CARS Rule was originally set to take effect on July 30, 2024. However, the FTC postponed the effective date to Sept. 30, 2025.
On Jan. 27, 2025, the Fifth Circuit ruled 2-1 to vacate the CARS Rule on procedural grounds.

The court rejected the FTC's argument that it had promulgated the CARS Rule under the Dodd-Frank Act, finding instead that "the Dodd-Frank Act does not grant the [FTC] any substantive authority separate from § 18(a)(1)(B) of the FTC Act."
As the CARS Rule is effectively nullified, the FTC must now revisit the rulemaking process.

A reasonable expectation shall include any expectation recognized by law, including, but not limited to, an expectation derived from a privilege, immunity, or right established by common law, Supreme Court rule, or the Illinois or United States Constitution.`  `(e) Private electronic communication.` `For purposes of this Article, "private electronic communication" means any transfer of signs, signals, writing, images, sounds, data, or intelligence of any nature transmitted in whole or part by a wire, radio, pager, computer, electromagnetic, photo electronic or photo optical system, when the sending or receiving party intends the electronic communication to be private under circumstances reasonably justifying that expectation. citations.title: Enforcement Actions | Consumer Financial Protection Bureau
excerpts: When we take an enforcement action against an entity or person we believe has violated the law, we will post court documents and other related materials here.
The Bureau may enforce the law by filing an action in federal district court or by initiating an administrative adjudication proceeding.
Administrative proceedings are conducted by an Administrative Law Judge, who holds hearings and issues a recommended decision.
On August 21, 2025, the Bureau commenced an adversary proceeding and filed a complaint and proposed stipulated final judgment and order against Synapse Financial Technologies, Inc.
On January 30, 2025, the Bureau issued an order against Wise US Inc., a nonbank remittance transfer provider headquartered in New York, New York.
On January 17, 2025, the Bureau issued an order against Equifax, Inc. and Equifax Information Services LLC, one of the largest consumer reporting agencies in the country.
On January 14, 2025, the Bureau filed a lawsuit against Capital One, N.A., a national bank with over $480 billion in assets, and its parent holding company, Capital One Financial Corp.
On October 23, 2024, the Bureau issued an order against Apple Inc.

outputBasis.reasoning:
The provided value for 'decision_criteria' outlines several key factors that influence a Dealer Principal's technology purchase decisions. These include a clear Return on Investment (ROI) with a quantifiable payback timeline, assurance of legal and risk compliance (including data privacy and recording consent laws), minimal operational disruption, seamless integration with existing systems (DMS, CRM), strong vendor references, a proven track record, and robust contractual terms with security certifications like SOC 2. 

Several excerpts directly address these points:

Excerpts discussing ROI and cost reduction highlight the financial justification for technology adoption. For instance, one excerpt mentions how a tool can "cut down the deal review time from 30 minutes to a fraction" and "reduce the risk of error with effective automation," directly relating to cost savings and efficiency. Another notes that AI can "free your team to focus on selling cars and product instead of chasing paper and worrying about compliance issues," indicating a focus on revenue generation and cost reduction.

Excerpts detailing legal and risk posture are crucial. Information on compliance with FTC regulations, state-specific laws regarding recording conversations (like California's two-party consent and Illinois' two-party consent), and data privacy laws (like CCPA) are directly relevant. The mention of "mitigate compliance risks" and "ensure 100% compliance" also speaks to this criterion.

Integration with existing systems is addressed by excerpts that mention "seamless integration" and connecting with "dealership management systems (DMS), CRM platforms, and scheduling tools." The need for tools that "work with their people and systems" also aligns with this point.

Vendor reputation and trust are supported by mentions of "robust contractual terms," "security certifications (like SOC 2 Type 2)," and "proven track record." The emphasis on "enterprise-ready" security and compliance, along with specific certifications, builds confidence in a vendor.

Finally, the overall focus on "outcomes they can measure—more cars sold, lower inventory costs, higher gross profit" and delivering "results, not just pushing technology" encapsulates the strategic and financial drivers for technology adoption by dealerships.

executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of the findings, summarizing the market need, feasibility, and the significant legal, technical, and business challenges for the proposed AI-powered car buying app.

overall_feasibility_assessment.description: A conclusive assessment of the overall viability of the proposed application in 2026, synthesizing the key technical enablers, market opportunities, and critical risks.

feature_name.description: The name of the feature being analyzed.

pain_point.description: A specific pain point or problem that the feature addresses for consumers or dealers.

supporting_evidence.description: Data, consumer complaint records, or regulatory actions (e.g., FTC warnings) that back the existence of the pain point.

target_user.description: The primary user type (e.g., Car Buyer, Salesperson) who experiences this pain point.

feature_pain_points_solved.description: A detailed analysis for each of the four features (F&I add-on scanner, Deal decoder, Live objection coach, Customer intel logger) outlining the specific consumer and dealer pain points they address, backed by evidence from regulatory actions, consumer complaints, and market data.

feature_name.description: The name of the feature being analyzed.

comparable_tool_category.description: The category of existing tools (e.g., Dealer DMS, Conversation Analytics).

existing_tools_summary.description: A summary of what current tools in this category do.

identified_gap.description: The specific capability that existing tools lack, representing a market opportunity for the new app.

comparable_tools_and_market_gaps.description: An evaluation of existing comparable tools for each of the four features, detailing their current capabilities and identifying the specific market gaps and differentiation opportunities for the proposed app.

feature_name.description: The name of the feature being analyzed.

technical_component.description: A specific technical component required, such as Audio Front-end, Real-time STT, NLP, or Integration.

details.description: Specific details and requirements for the technical component.

performance_target.description: Key performance indicators or targets, such as latency in milliseconds.

feature_technical_requirements.description: A breakdown of the specific technical requirements to build each of the four features, including details on speech recognition (ASR), natural language processing (NLP), data storage, system integrations, and recommended architecture.

feature_name.description: The name of the feature being analyzed.

legal_area.description: The specific area of legal or privacy concern, such as Recording Consent, Data Security, or Consumer Protection.

consideration_details.description: A detailed description of the legal or privacy consideration and its implications.

relevant_laws.description: Specific laws or regulations that apply, such as GLBA, BIPA, or state penal codes.

feature_legal_and_privacy_considerations.description: An analysis of the unique legal, privacy, and compliance considerations for each of the four features, focusing on conversation recording, data storage, consumer protection law, and PII handling.

feature_name.description: The name of the feature being analyzed.

user_type.description: The user type being audited (e.g., Car Buyer, Salesperson).

workflow_description.description: Description of the steps the user must take to use the feature.

potential_friction_point.description: A specific point in the workflow that could cause friction or difficulty for the user.

mitigation_strategy.description: A recommended strategy to reduce or eliminate the identified friction.

user_friction_and_workflow_audit.description: An audit of the steps a car buyer or salesperson would need to take to use each of the four features, identifying potential sources of friction and recommending mitigation strategies for a smooth user experience.

feature_name.description: The name of the feature being analyzed.

value_proposition.description: The core value or reason why a customer would pay for this feature (e.g., reduce litigation risk, increase conversion).

target_buyer.description: The primary B2B buyer persona for this feature (e.g., Dealer, OEM).

suggested_pricing_model.description: The recommended pricing model, such as B2B SaaS, per-deal, or freemium.

feature_monetization_and_pricing.description: An exploration of the value proposition and potential monetization strategies for each of the four features, including what would motivate someone to pay and what pricing models (e.g., SaaS, per-deal) are most suitable.

enforcement_stance_summary.description: A summary of the FTC's current enforcement posture on auto retail pricing and add-ons.

cars_rule_status.description: The current litigation and regulatory status of the FTC's CARS Rule as of the research date.

implications_for_app.description: How the FTC's posture affects the viability and design of a buyer-assistive app.

vendor_liability_notes.description: Key considerations for the app vendor to avoid legal liability.

state.description: The U.S. state being analyzed (e.g., California, Illinois, Texas).

consent_requirement.description: The type of consent required for recording conversations (e.g., All-party, One-party).

statute_reference.description: The legal statute or code that governs recording consent in the state.

key_implications.description: Specific implications for the app, such as biometric privacy rules (BIPA) or the need for explicit signage.

performance_metric.description: The metric being assessed, such as accuracy (Word Error Rate) or latency.

benchmark_value.description: The benchmark value for the metric in a given environment (e.g., 70-85% accuracy in noisy environments).

hardware_considerations.description: Recommendations for hardware, such as microphone arrays or edge devices, to achieve target performance.

model_recommendations.description: Recommendations for ASR models, including domain-tuning and custom vocabulary needs.

buyer_persona.description: The title of the B2B buyer persona (e.g., Dealer Principal, Group Compliance Officer, General Sales Manager).

primary_goals.description: The main business objectives and goals of this buyer persona.

key_pain_points.description: The primary challenges and pain points this buyer faces.

decision_criteria.description: The key factors this buyer considers when making a purchasing decision, such as ROI and legal posture.

architecture_model.description: The recommended architectural model (e.g., Hybrid, Edge-First).

edge_components.description: Components and processing that should occur on an on-site edge gateway.

cloud_components.description: Components and processing that can be handled in the cloud.

rationale.description: The reasoning for recommending this architecture, balancing accuracy, latency, cost, and privacy.

risk_category.description: The category of the risk (e.g., Legal/Privacy, Technical, Cultural Resistance, Adoption).

risk_description.description: A detailed description of the specific risk.

mitigation_strategy.description: An actionable strategy to mitigate or manage the identified risk.

severity.description: The assessed severity of the risk (e.g., High, Medium, Low).

research_area.description: The specific area recommended for follow-up research.

objective.description: The goal or question that the follow-up research aims to answer.

recommended_actions.description: Specific, actionable steps to conduct the research, such as conducting a pilot or engaging legal counsel.
